channel.title: New on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in New on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc - out of memory with new
item.description:
std::bad_alloc - out of memory with new

std::bad_alloc is thrown when the new operator fails to allocate memory. The system has run out of available memory, or the requested allocation exceeds available resources.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Excessively large allocation
int* arr = new int[10000000000LL]; // may throw std::bad_alloc

// Cause 2: Memory leak accumulation
while (true) {
 new int[1000]; // leaks memory
}

// Cause 3: Fragmented heap
// Total free memory exists but not contiguous

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use std::nothrow

int* arr = new(std::nothrow) int[100000000];
if (arr == nullptr) {
 std::cerr << "Allocation failed" << std::endl;
 return 1;
}

Fix 2: Catch and handle

try {
 int* arr = new int[100000000];
 delete[] arr;
} catch (const std::bad_alloc& e) {
 std::cerr << "Memory allocation failed: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 return 1;
}

Fix 3: Use smart pointers

auto arr = std::make_unique<int[]>(100000000);
if (!arr) {
 std::cerr << "Allocation failed" << std::endl;
}

Related Errors

  • std::bad_array_new_length — invalid array size.
  • std::length_error — container resize too large.
  • Memory leak: Valgrind — gradual memory exhaustion.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc — Memory Allocation Failed Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc — Memory Allocation Failed Fix

A std::bad_alloc is thrown when the new operator or malloc() fails to allocate the requested amount of memory. This typically happens when the system runs out of available memory, the requested size is unreasonably large, or there is a memory leak gradually exhausting available resources. The exception is defined in <new>.

Common Causes

  • Requesting an excessively large allocation — new int[10000000000] exceeds available memory
  • Memory leak accumulation — repeated allocations without deallocation exhaust memory over time
  • Fragmented heap — total free memory exists but is not contiguous
  • Too many threads — each thread’s stack consumes memory, reducing heap availability

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use std::nothrow to avoid exceptions

#include <iostream>
#include <new>

int main() {
 int* arr = new(std::nothrow) int[100000000];
 if (arr == nullptr) {
 std::cerr << "Allocation failed — out of memory" << std::endl;
 return 1;
 }

 /* use arr ... */
 delete[] arr;
 return 0;
}

Fix 2: Catch and handle bad_alloc

#include <iostream>
#include <new>
#include <vector>

int main() {
 try {
 std::vector<int> v;
 v.resize(10000000000ULL); // may throw std::bad_alloc
 } catch (const std::bad_alloc& e) {
 std::cerr << "Memory allocation failed: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 return 1;
 }
 return 0;
}

Fix 3: Validate size before allocating

#include <iostream>
#include <new>
#include <limits>

template <typename T>
T* safe_allocate(size_t count) {
 if (count > std::numeric_limits<size_t>::max() / sizeof(T)) {
 std::cerr << "Allocation size would overflow" << std::endl;
 return nullptr;
 }
 return new(std::nothrow) T[count];
}

int main() {
 int* p = safe_allocate<int>(100);
 if (p) {
 std::cout << "Allocated successfully" << std::endl;
 delete[] p;
 }
 return 0;
}

Fix 4: Use memory pools for frequent small allocations

#include <iostream>
#include <vector>
#include <memory>

class ObjectPool {
public:
 ObjectPool(size_t pool_size) : pool_(pool_size) {
 for (size_t i = 0; i < pool_size; i++) {
 free_.push_back(i);
 }
 }

 int allocate() {
 if (free_.empty()) return -1;
 int idx = free_.back();
 free_.pop_back();
 return idx;
 }

 void deallocate(int idx) {
 free_.push_back(idx);
 }

private:
 std::vector<int> pool_;
 std::vector<int> free_;
};

int main() {
 ObjectPool pool(1000);
 int obj = pool.allocate();
 std::cout << "Allocated object at index " << obj << std::endl;
 pool.deallocate(obj);
 return 0;
}

Examples

#include <iostream>
#include <new>

int main() {
 /* Huge allocation that will likely fail */
 try {
 int* huge = new int[10000000000LL];
 delete[] huge;
 } catch (const std::bad_alloc& e) {
 std::cerr << "Cannot allocate: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 }

 /* Using new(std::nothrow) for safe failure */
 int* p = new(std::nothrow) int[10000000000LL];
 if (!p) {
 std::cerr << "Allocation returned nullptr" << std::endl;
 }

 return 0;
}

Related Errors

  • std::length_error — container resize exceeds max allowed size
  • std::bad_array_length — invalid array length in new[]
  • Memory Leak: Valgrind Lost Bytes — gradual memory exhaustion

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc — Memory Allocation Failure Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc — Memory Allocation Failure Fix

A std::bad_alloc is thrown when operator new fails to allocate the requested memory. This typically happens when the system runs out of memory or the process exceeds its memory limits. All dynamic memory allocations using new, new[], and containers like std::vector can throw this exception.

Why std::bad_alloc Occurs

Common causes include requesting more memory than is available, excessive memory fragmentation preventing large contiguous allocations, memory leaks exhausting available memory, and process memory limits being reached.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_array_new_length - invalid array size
item.description:
std::bad_array_new_length - invalid array size

std::bad_array_new_length is thrown when new[] receives an array length that is zero or exceeds implementation limits.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Zero-length array
int* arr = new int[0]; // throws

// Cause 2: Negative length
int* arr = new int[-5]; // throws (converted to huge unsigned)

// Cause 3: Overflow in size calculation
size_t count = SIZE_MAX / sizeof(int) + 1;
int* arr = new int[count]; // overflow

How to Fix

Fix 1: Check length before allocation

size_t n = get_count();
if (n == 0) {
 // handle zero case
}
int* arr = new int[n];

Fix 2: Use std::vector

std::vector<int> v(n); // safe — handles size validation

Fix 3: Catch and handle

try {
 int* arr = new int[n];
} catch (const std::bad_array_new_length& e) {
 std::cerr << "Invalid array length: " << e.what() << std::endl;
 return 1;
}

Related Errors

  • std::bad_alloc — allocation failure.
  • std::length_error — vector resize too large.
  • std::out_of_range — index out of range.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_array_new_length — Array Size Validation Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::bad_array_new_length — Array Size Validation Fix

A std::bad_array_new_length is thrown when you attempt to allocate an array using new[] with an invalid size — such as a negative number, zero (for some types), or a size that causes internal arithmetic overflow. This exception inherits from std::bad_alloc and was introduced in C++11.

Why std::bad_array_new_length Occurs

Common causes include passing a negative value (converted to a large unsigned) to new[], requesting new T[0] for non-default-constructible types, and size overflow when multiplying element count by element size.

item.title: [Solution] Pascal: heap memory error
item.description:
What This Error Means

Pascal heap errors occur when memory allocation fails, memory is accessed after being freed, or there are memory leaks from unfreed allocations.

Common Causes

  • Out of heap memory
  • Accessing freed memory
  • Memory leak (missing Dispose)
  • Double free
  • Invalid pointer operations

How to Fix

program HeapError;
var
 p: ^Integer;
begin
 New(p);
 Dispose(p);
 p^ := 42; // Error: accessing freed memory
end.

program SafeHeap;
var
 p: ^Integer;
begin
 New(p);
 try
 p^ := 42;
 WriteLn(p^);
 finally
 Dispose(p);
 end;
end.

program HeapAllocation;
var
 arr: array of Integer;
 i: Integer;
begin
 SetLength(arr, 1000); // Allocates on heap
 
 for i := 0 to High(arr) do
 arr[i] := i;
 
 // Memory automatically freed when arr goes out of scope
end.

program MemoryLeak;
var
 p: ^Integer;
begin
 p := New(p);
 p^ := 100;
 // Forgot Dispose(p) - memory leak!
end.

program SafeMemory;
var
 p: ^Integer;
begin
 p := nil;
 
 New(p);
 try
 p^ := 100;
 WriteLn(p^);
 finally
 if p <> nil then
 begin
 Dispose(p);
 p := nil;
 end;
 end;
end.

program ArrayHeap;
var
 matrix: array of array of Integer;
 i, j: Integer;
begin
 SetLength(matrix, 10, 10);
 
 for i := 0 to 9 do
 for j := 0 to 9 do
 matrix[i][j] := i * j;
 
 // Use matrix...
 
 SetLength(matrix, 0); // Free memory
end.

Related Errors

  • Invalid Pointer - pointer errors
  • Stack Overflow - stack errors
  • Runtime Error - general runtime

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2351 — This Expression Is Not Constructable
item.description:
TS2351 — This expression is not constructable

TS2351 occurs when you try to use new on a value whose type doesn’t have a construct signature. This happens when calling new on a function that isn’t a constructor, an object, or a primitive.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using 'new' on a regular function
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
const g = new greet("Alice"); // TS2351: not constructable

// Cause 2: Using 'new' on an arrow function
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create(); // TS2351

// Cause 3: Using 'new' on a module default export that isn't a class
import utils from "./utils";
const u = new utils(); // TS2351

// Cause 4: Using 'new' on an interface
interface Factory {
 create(): Widget;
}
const f = new Factory(); // TS2351: interfaces aren't values

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a class with a constructor

// Wrong — regular function
function Greeter(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // TS2351

// Correct — use a class
class Greeter {
 name: string;
 constructor(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
 }
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use factory function without ’new'

// Wrong
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create();

// Correct — call without 'new'
const obj = create();

Fix 3: Import the class correctly

// Wrong — importing non-class default export
import utils from "./utils";
new utils();

// Correct — import a class
import { MyClass } from "./my-class";
new MyClass();

Fix 4: Define a construct signature for the type

interface Constructor {
 new (name: string): { name: string };
}

function createInstance(Ctor: Constructor, name: string) {
 return new Ctor(name); // OK
}

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'new' on a function stored in a variable
const initialize = function () {};
const app = new initialize(); // TS2351

// Example 2: Using 'new' on a namespace
namespace Utils {
 export function helper() {}
}
new Utils(); // TS2351

Related Errors

  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — trying to call () on non-callable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — property access issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2673 — X cannot be instantiated
item.description:
TS2673 — Member ‘X’ cannot be instantiated with ’new’

TS2673 occurs when you try to use new on an abstract class or a type that does not have a construct signature.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Instantiating abstract class
abstract class Shape {
 abstract area(): number;
}
const s = new Shape(); // TS2673

// Cause 2: Interface without construct signature
interface Factory {
 create(): void;
}
const f = new Factory(); // TS2673

// Cause 3: Type alias not constructable
type Point = { x: number; y: number };
const p = new Point(); // TS2673

How to Fix

Fix 1: Instantiate concrete subclass

class Circle extends Shape {
 area() { return Math.PI * 4; }
}
const c = new Circle();

Fix 2: Add construct signature

interface Factory {
 new (): { create(): void };
}

Fix 3: Use object literal

const p: Point = { x: 1, y: 2 };

Related Errors

  • TS2511: Cannot create instance of abstract class — abstract instantiation.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist — property access errors.
  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — non-callable expression.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc — Memory Allocation Failed Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::bad_alloc — Memory Allocation Failed Fix

A std::bad_alloc exception is thrown when the C++ runtime cannot allocate the requested memory using new, new[], or std::vector::resize(). This typically means the system has exhausted its available memory or the process has hit its resource limits.

Why std::bad_alloc Occurs

Common causes include allocating very large arrays, requesting more memory than the system has, memory fragmentation on embedded systems, or a combination of small allocations that accumulate over time.

item.title: [Solution] C++ std::bad_array_new_length — Invalid Array Allocation Fix
item.description:
[Solution] C++ std::bad_array_new_length — Invalid Array Allocation Fix

A std::bad_array_new_length is thrown when you attempt to allocate an array with a negative size, zero elements (for types without a non-throwing default constructor in some implementations), or a size that would cause an internal arithmetic overflow. This exception was introduced in C++11 and is a subclass of std::bad_alloc.

Why std::bad_array_new_length Occurs

Common causes include requesting new T[negative_number], requesting new T[0] for non-default-constructible types, and size calculations that overflow before the allocation call.
